title: Alnylam — AHP — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: GIVLAARI (givosiran) in Acute Hepatic Porphyria

Product: GIVLAARI (givosiran) Manufacturer: Alnylam Pharmaceuticals Indication: Treatment of adults with acute hepatic porphyria (AHP) Document scope: Disease landscape, product profile, market dynamics, patient journey Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 What Is Acute Hepatic Porphyria?

Acute hepatic porphyria (AHP) is a family of rare, genetic metabolic disorders caused by enzymatic defects in the heme biosynthesis pathway in the liver. These defects lead to accumulation of neurotoxic intermediates -- aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and porphobilinogen (PBG) -- which cause debilitating acute attacks, chronic symptoms, and long-term organ damage.

AHP is not a single disease but a group of four subtypes, each caused by a deficiency in a different enzyme in the heme pathway:

Subtype Abbreviation Enzyme Deficiency % of AHP Cases
Acute intermittent porphyria AIP Hydroxymethylbilane synthase (HMBS) ~80%
Variegate porphyria VP Protoporphyrinogen oxidase (PPOX) ~10%
Hereditary coproporphyria HCP Coproporphyrinogen oxidase (CPOX) ~8%
ALA dehydratase-deficiency porphyria ADP ALA dehydratase <1% (extremely rare)

Key biological concept: All four subtypes share the same upstream pathophysiology -- overproduction of ALA and PBG by hepatic ALAS1 (aminolevulinic acid synthase 1). This is the target that GIVLAARI addresses via RNA interference.

1.2 Epidemiology

AHP is an ultra-rare disease with complex epidemiology because the genetic prevalence is vastly higher than the clinical prevalence:

  • Genetic prevalence (pathogenic variants): 1 in 1,300 to 1 in 1,785 carry pathogenic variants for AIP based on population-level genetic studies -- far higher than previously believed
  • Clinical prevalence (symptomatic): ~1 in 100,000 experience symptoms. Most carriers never develop clinical disease (low penetrance, estimated 10-20%)
  • Annual symptomatic patients: ~5,000 people in the US and Europe experience one or more AHP attacks annually
  • Recurrent attacks: A smaller subset (~500-1,000 in the US) experience recurrent attacks (4+ per year) requiring intervention -- this is the core GIVLAARI treatment population
  • Demographics: ~90% of symptomatic patients are women of reproductive age, with most attacks occurring between ages 15-45. Hormonal fluctuations (menstrual cycle, pregnancy) are key triggers

Critical insight for Alnylam's commercial challenge: The gap between genetic prevalence (~1 in 1,500) and clinical prevalence (~1 in 100,000) represents the massive underdiagnosis problem. The commercial opportunity is not about beating competitors -- it is about finding undiagnosed patients and expanding the diagnosed patient pool.

1.3 Clinical Presentation

AHP manifests along a spectrum that ranges from acute attacks to chronic daily symptoms to long-term organ damage:

Acute Attacks:

  • Severe, diffuse abdominal pain (hallmark symptom, present in >90% of attacks)
  • Nausea, vomiting, constipation
  • Neurological symptoms: peripheral neuropathy, muscle weakness, seizures
  • Cardiovascular: tachycardia, hypertension
  • Psychiatric: anxiety, confusion, hallucinations, acute psychosis
  • Severe cases: respiratory paralysis, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), hyponatremia
  • Attacks last days to weeks; mean hospitalization across patients: 82 days over ~21 years of observation

Chronic Symptoms (between attacks):

  • Chronic neuropathic pain (requiring weekly analgesics in many patients)
  • Fatigue, nausea
  • Anxiety, depression
  • Reduced quality of life
  • Most HCPs now recognize AHP as a predominantly chronic condition, not merely episodic

Long-Term Complications (from sustained elevated ALA/PBG):

  • Chronic kidney disease (CKD)
  • Peripheral neuropathy
  • Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) -- lifetime risk significantly elevated
  • Hypertension
  • Chronic pain syndromes

Evolving HCP understanding: A key ZoomRx ATU finding is that US HCPs increasingly view AHP as a chronic condition where patients experience chronic symptoms between episodic attacks (majority agreement in Wave 4, 2024). Their primary treatment objective has shifted toward preventing/reducing long-term complications from elevated ALA/PBG, not just preventing attacks. This reframing is central to GIVLAARI's expanded value proposition beyond attack prevention.

1.4 The Diagnostic Journey -- The Core Commercial Challenge

The diagnosis of AHP is notoriously delayed. This diagnostic journey is the single biggest barrier to GIVLAARI market growth:

  • Average diagnostic delay: Up to 15 years from symptom onset to correct diagnosis
  • Misdiagnosis rate: 26% of AHP patients initially receive a wrong diagnosis
  • Common misdiagnoses: IBS, Crohn's disease, endometriosis, fibromyalgia, psychiatric disorders, appendicitis (leading to unnecessary surgeries)

Why diagnosis is so difficult:

  1. Symptom mimicry: Abdominal pain, nausea, and neurological symptoms overlap with dozens of common conditions
  2. Rarity: Most physicians will never see an AHP patient in their career, so it is not on their differential
  3. Episodic nature: Patients may be asymptomatic between attacks, and symptoms resolve before porphyria testing is considered
  4. Testing complexity: Correct diagnosis requires spot urine for ALA/PBG (not just porphyrins). Inappropriate testing (porphyrins only), improper sample handling, and ordering genetic testing as first-line all create obstacles
  5. Multiple specialties involved: Patients bounce between GI, gynecology, neurology, psychiatry, and emergency medicine before reaching a porphyria specialist
  6. Low awareness among non-specialists: PCPs, ER physicians, and general gastroenterologists rarely think of AHP

Referral patterns (from ZoomRx Global ATU Wave 4):

  • US: 59% of AHP patients are referred from other specialties, primarily general/family practitioners, PCPs, or gastroenterologists
  • Brazil: 61% referred from other specialties
  • Germany: 47% referred (lower, suggesting more direct specialist identification)

Implication: Alnylam's commercial success depends more on disease education and diagnostic awareness campaigns than on competitive messaging. Every newly diagnosed patient is a potential GIVLAARI patient because there are no real alternatives for prophylactic treatment.

1.5 Attack Triggers

Common triggers that precipitate acute attacks include:

  • Hormonal fluctuations (menstrual cycle -- most common trigger in women)
  • Fasting or caloric restriction
  • Medications (barbiturates, sulfonamides, some anticonvulsants, hormonal contraceptives)
  • Alcohol consumption
  • Stress (physical or emotional)
  • Infections
  • Smoking

2. Product Profiles

2.1 GIVLAARI (givosiran) -- The Only Approved Prophylactic Therapy

Mechanism of action: GIVLAARI is an RNAi (RNA interference) therapeutic that specifically targets and degrades ALAS1 mRNA in hepatocytes. ALAS1 is the rate-limiting enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway -- its upregulation drives the overproduction of neurotoxic ALA and PBG. By silencing ALAS1, GIVLAARI reduces circulating ALA and PBG levels, preventing both acute attacks and the chronic toxicity from sustained metabolite elevation.

The siRNA is conjugated to a GalNAc (N-acetylgalactosamine) ligand that enables targeted delivery to hepatocytes via asialoglycoprotein receptors (ASGPRs). This is the same GalNAc-conjugation platform used across Alnylam's RNAi portfolio.

Key clinical data (ENVISION Phase 3 trial):

  • Primary endpoint: 74% mean reduction in annualized composite porphyria attack rate vs. placebo (p<0.001) at 6 months
  • Attack-free patients: 50% of GIVLAARI patients were attack-free at 6 months vs. 17% on placebo
  • Hemin use reduction: 70% reduction in average days of hemin use vs. placebo
  • ALA/PBG reduction: Median 83.7% reduction in urinary ALA and 75.1% reduction in PBG within 14 days of first dose; maximal reductions (~94%) by Month 3, sustained with continued dosing
  • Long-term data (OLE): Sustained attack reduction, continued hemin use reduction, and improved quality of life with acceptable safety profile over 12+ months

Dosing: 2.5 mg/kg subcutaneous injection once monthly. A missed dose should be given as soon as possible, then monthly thereafter.

Safety profile:

  • Most common adverse reactions (>= 20%): Nausea (27%), injection site reactions (25%)
  • Key monitored AEs: Transaminase elevations (ALT >= 3x ULN in 15% of patients in pivotal trial), renal-related AEs requiring GFR monitoring, elevated homocysteine levels
  • Contraindicated in patients with known severe hypersensitivity to givosiran (anaphylaxis reported)
  • Requires liver function test (LFT) monitoring

Regulatory approvals:

  • US FDA: November 2019 (adults with AHP)
  • EU (European Commission): March 2020 (adults and adolescents 12+ with AHP)
  • Brazil: Approved (date varies by regulatory pathway)

Pricing:

  • US list price: ~$39,000 per vial
  • Annual treatment cost: ~$575,000 at list; ~$442,000 after discounts
  • Alnylam offers prevalence-based value agreements: if a payer has more patients than expected, Alnylam provides rebates; in exchange, payers cover GIVLAARI based on FDA label rather than restrictive clinical trial criteria

2.2 PANHEMATIN / NORMOSANG (Hemin) -- The Only Other AHP-Specific Treatment

Mechanism: Hemin (heme for injection) replenishes the depleted heme pool in hepatocytes, providing negative feedback to ALAS1 and reducing ALA/PBG production. It treats the biochemical derangement but does not prevent attacks prophylactically.

Approved indication: PANHEMATIN (US) is indicated for the amelioration of recurrent attacks of acute intermittent porphyria related to the menstrual cycle in susceptible women, after initial carbohydrate therapy is inadequate. FDA has designated it an "essential medicine."

Clinical use patterns (from ZoomRx data):

  • As needed (acute treatment): Used during attacks to reduce severity and duration. This is the most common use, particularly in Brazil
  • Prophylactic (off-label): Some HCPs use regularly scheduled hemin infusions to prevent attacks. This off-label use is more common in the US
  • PANHEMATIN holds ~56% of total patient share in the US across both as-needed and prophylactic use (Wave 4, 2024)

Key limitations vs. GIVLAARI:

  • Requires IV administration, often in hospital/infusion center setting
  • No subcutaneous option -- creates access and convenience barriers
  • Associated with phlebitis, catheter complications, iron overload with repeated use
  • Not approved for prophylactic use (off-label)
  • Tachyphylaxis may develop with repeated use

Competitive dynamic: GIVLAARI is gaining share primarily from prophylactic PANHEMATIN users and treatment-naive patients. PANHEMATIN as-needed retains a role for acute attack management and may complement GIVLAARI.

2.3 Other Management Approaches

  • IV glucose/carbohydrate loading: First-line acute management for mild attacks; suppresses ALAS1 via metabolic feedback. Inadequate for moderate-to-severe attacks
  • Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues: Used in some women with menstrual-cycle-related attacks to suppress ovulation
  • Liver transplantation: Curative but reserved for extremely severe, refractory cases with life-threatening complications
  • Supportive care: Pain management (opioids, often chronic), anti-emetics, seizure management (levetiracetam preferred -- many anticonvulsants are contraindicated)

3. Market Dynamics

3.1 This Is Not a Competitive Market -- It Is a Patient-Finding Market

The fundamental commercial challenge for GIVLAARI is unlike any typical pharma competitive scenario:

  • No direct competitor: GIVLAARI is essentially first-in-class for prophylactic AHP treatment. There is no competing RNAi or targeted therapy in development for AHP
  • Indirect competitor (hemin): PANHEMATIN/NORMOSANG is an IV treatment used acutely and sometimes prophylactically, but it has significant convenience and safety disadvantages
  • Pipeline: The AHP pipeline is sparse. No novel mechanisms are in late-stage development as of 2026
  • The real "competitor" is non-diagnosis: The vast majority of potential GIVLAARI patients have not been diagnosed with AHP. Every new diagnosis expands the addressable market

3.2 Market Size and Revenue

  • GIVLAARI + OXLUMO combined revenue: $500 million in FY2025 (up 18% from FY2024)
  • Q4 2025 combined revenue: $137 million (up 26% YoY)
  • GIVLAARI patient growth: ~15% year-over-year in 2025
  • 2026 guidance: Mid-single-digit growth for rare disease portfolio
  • Context: GIVLAARI is a mature rare disease franchise within Alnylam's portfolio. The company's aggressive growth targets (25% CAGR through 2030) are driven by the TTR amyloidosis franchise (AMVUTTRA/ONPATTRO), not GIVLAARI

3.3 Regional Market Differences

ZoomRx runs both US PET and Global ATU (US, Germany, Brazil) for GIVLAARI, providing rich cross-market data:

United States (n=73 HCPs, Wave 4):

  • Largest market with most established diagnostic infrastructure
  • HCPs increasingly view AHP as a chronic condition (majority in W4)
  • Primary treatment objective: preventing long-term complications from elevated ALA/PBG (shift from attack prevention)
  • GIVLAARI top driver: Efficacy in improving chronic symptoms (59%)
  • GIVLAARI top barriers: High OOP costs (56%), payer coverage concerns (53%)
  • GIVLAARI patient share expected to grow +8% in next 12 months
  • GIVLAARI preferred across all age groups (no age exclusion)
  • 66% community practice, 34% academic
  • HCP segmentation: Enthusiasts (11%, high load/high share), Trialists (21%, low load/high share), Converters (22%, high load/low share), Inexperienced (47%, low load/low share) -- most volume driven by 32% who are Enthusiasts + Trialists

Germany (n=25 HCPs, Wave 4):

  • Highest current GIVLAARI patient share across all regions
  • GIVLAARI usage increased ~14% in Q3 2024
  • Treatment focus shifted from purely LTC prevention to balanced approach (attacks + LTC)
  • GIVLAARI top drivers: Ability to reduce attacks (64%), targeted MOA (41%)
  • GIVLAARI top barriers: Patient refusal (41%), burden of attacks not outweighing monthly dosing (41%)
  • Barriers related to payer coverage and inclusion criteria significantly decreased
  • HCPs more sensitive to administration logistic burden
  • Patient age is a bigger factor -- GIVLAARI preferred for younger patients (18-30)
  • Proximity to medical care has limited/no influence (48% of HCPs)
  • GIVLAARI share expected to grow +9% in next 12 months
  • 68% academic, 32% community

Brazil (n=15 HCPs, Wave 4):

  • Smallest market in the study with significant access challenges
  • HCPs less likely to consider AHP events without medical intervention as true attacks
  • HCPs prioritize attack prevention over LTC management (different from US)
  • GIVLAARI top driver: Ability to significantly reduce attacks (75%)
  • GIVLAARI top barriers: High OOP costs (58%), payer coverage (58%)
  • ~45% of patients not on consistent treatment due to cost/payer restrictions
  • Proximity to medical care is a significant influencing factor (40% of HCPs)
  • GIVLAARI preferred for younger patients (18-30) but hemin as needed preferred for 46+
  • GIVLAARI patient share expected to remain consistent (~26%)
  • Cost/access is a structural barrier, not just a commercial one

3.4 HCP Segmentation (US Detail)

ZoomRx ATU Wave 4 identified four distinct HCP segments based on AHP patient load and GIVLAARI patient share:

Segment % of HCPs AHP Patient Load GIVLAARI Share Key Characteristics
Enthusiasts 11% High (>=7) High (>=38%) Go-to therapy, confident in chronic symptom efficacy. Biggest barrier: payer coverage/inclusion criteria
Trialists 21% Low (<7) High (>=38%) Favor GIVLAARI despite smaller load. Value chronic symptom/attack reduction. Biggest barrier: high OOP costs
Converters 22% High (>=7) Low (<38%) Bought into prophylactic therapy but question GIVLAARI's superior efficacy vs. hemin. Need education on clinical trial data
Inexperienced 47% Low (<7) Low (<38%) Mostly prescribe hemin as needed. Like GIVLAARI's MOA but limited by payer coverage and patients not meeting criteria

Strategic implication: The 47% Inexperienced segment represents the largest conversion opportunity. These HCPs manage few AHP patients and default to hemin as needed. Alnylam's field force should educate them on GIVLAARI's benefits for patients who present with even a single attack, moving beyond the "4+ attacks" mental threshold.

3.5 Patient Types Framework

ZoomRx tracks GIVLAARI usage across five defined patient types:

Patient Type Definition GIVLAARI Affinity
Type 1: Recurrent Attacks 4+ attacks requiring healthcare intervention in past 12 months Highest GIVLAARI share across all regions -- the core treated population
Type 2: Severe History, <2 attacks/year At least 1 severe/life-threatening attack in past 2 years, but <=2 in last 12 months Moderate GIVLAARI share; clinical uncertainty about prophylaxis
Type 3: Typical History, <2 attacks/year At least 1 non-severe attack in past 2 years, <=2 in last 12 months Lowest prophylactic therapy use; most likely on hemin as needed
Type 4: Chronic Symptoms No attacks in past 2 years, but chronic AHP symptoms affecting QoL Growing GIVLAARI share -- this is the expansion frontier, especially in US
Type 5: Biochemically Active No attacks or known chronic symptoms, but elevated ALA/PBG Lowest treatment rates; debated whether prophylaxis is warranted

Expansion direction: GIVLAARI's growth trajectory depends on moving beyond Patient Type 1 (recurrent attackers) into Type 2, Type 4, and eventually Type 5. The shift in disease understanding from "episodic attack disease" to "chronic disease with biochemical toxicity" supports this expansion.

4. Promotional Landscape (from PET Data)

4.1 Message Effectiveness

ZoomRx PET tracks GIVLAARI message effectiveness, recall, and motivation to prescribe. Key findings (Q2 2023):

Most effective messages (above rare disease industry average of 58%):

  • GIV08 (Hemin use reduction): "GIVLAARI reduced average days of hemin use by 70% vs. placebo" -- highest recall and effectiveness
  • GIV05 (ALA/PBG reduction): "Rapid and sustained reductions in ALA and PBG" -- strong efficacy signal
  • GIV02 (Attack reduction): "Significant reduction in porphyria attacks" -- high motivation to Rx
  • GIV03 (Attack-free patients): "50% attack-free vs. 17% placebo" -- compelling comparative data
  • GIV06 (MOA): "RNAi targeting ALAS1 mRNA" -- appeals to scientifically oriented HCPs

Underperforming messages (below industry average for recall despite high motivation):

  • GIV11 (Daily worst pain scores) -- highly motivating but poorly recalled, suggesting delivery gap
  • GIV12 (Analgesic use reduction) -- same pattern

All GIVLAARI messages are rated above industry average for effectiveness in Q2 2023.

4.2 Sales Rep Performance

  • GIVLAARI reps rated highly above industry standards across all performance attributes
  • Higher proportion of MDs are promoters of Alnylam (NPS improved Q4 2022 to Q2 2023)
  • Over 50% of GIVLAARI sales calls are "high-impact details" in Q2 2023
  • In-person meetings are the most preferred mode of engagement
  • Efficacy discussions, product brochures, and rep knowledge of AHP drive high-impact interactions
  • Topics most discussed: efficacy (66%), indication, safety, dosing, patient types, AHP testing/diagnosis

4.3 Key Promotional Implication

Alnylam reps should prioritize:

  1. Leading with chronic symptom efficacy data (top driver in US) rather than just attack reduction
  2. Improving delivery of pain/analgesic data (GIV11, GIV12) which motivate Rx but have low recall
  3. Discussing AHP testing and diagnosis with gastroenterologists specifically (71% of GI docs discuss this vs. 7% of HemOncs)
  4. Targeting Converters and Inexperienced with clinical trial data (% of patients with 0 attacks) to overcome efficacy comparability concerns

5. Key Themes for ZoomRx Deliverables

5.1 Disease Reframing: Attacks to Chronicity

The most significant narrative shift in AHP is the reframing from "episodic attack disease" to "chronic disease with biochemical toxicity." This has direct implications for:

  • Expanding the treatable patient population (Types 4 and 5)
  • Supporting GIVLAARI's value beyond attack prevention
  • Driving long-term treatment adherence
  • Justifying treatment in patients with fewer attacks but elevated ALA/PBG

5.2 The Diagnostic Gap Is the Market Gap

Every patient found is a patient gained. Alnylam's growth depends on:

  • Educating PCPs, ER physicians, and GI specialists to test for AHP (urine ALA/PBG)
  • Reducing the average 15-year diagnostic delay
  • Genetic cascade testing of family members (supported by HCP belief that family members should be tested)
  • ZoomRx studies should continue tracking HCP referral patterns and diagnostic behaviors

5.3 Access as the Rate-Limiting Step

Even among diagnosed patients:

  • US: High OOP costs and payer coverage are top 2 barriers
  • Brazil: ~45% of patients not on consistent treatment due to cost
  • The attack-count inclusion criteria create a Catch-22: payers require 4+ attacks, but patients who would benefit may not yet have documented that many attacks
  • Alnylam's prevalence-based value agreements are a strategic response but need broader adoption

5.4 Global Heterogeneity

US, Germany, and Brazil represent three distinct market archetypes:

  • US: Largest market, most mature, shifting toward chronic disease management -- growth from Converter/Inexperienced HCP conversion
  • Germany: Highest GIVLAARI share, academic-dominated, growing share from new patient starts -- growth from continued clinical adoption
  • Brazil: Access-constrained, cost-dominated, attack-focused rather than chronicity-focused -- growth dependent on structural access improvements

6. ZoomRx Study Portfolio for GIVLAARI

Study Type Geography Frequency Latest Wave Key Deliverables
GIVLAARI Global ATU HCP ATU (Quant + Qual) US, Germany, Brazil Annual (Q3/Q4 fielding) Wave 4 (Sep-Oct 2024) Disease beliefs, treatment usage, drivers/barriers, HCP segmentation, future usage
GIVLAARI US PET Promotional Effectiveness Tracker US only Semi-annual Q2 2023 Message effectiveness, rep performance, NPS, promotional impact

Key contacts:

  • ZoomRx: Sneha Shah (Sr. Manager, primary contact), Richard Durante (Principal), Pranav UT (Sr. Consultant), Gauri Srivastava (Consultant), Sugandha Anand (Sr. Consultant)
  • Alnylam: Elizabeth Bergantino (Market Insights)

7. Glossary

Term Definition
AHP Acute Hepatic Porphyria -- umbrella term for 4 porphyria subtypes
AIP Acute Intermittent Porphyria -- most common AHP subtype (~80%)
ALA Aminolevulinic Acid -- neurotoxic heme precursor
PBG Porphobilinogen -- neurotoxic heme precursor
ALAS1 Aminolevulinic Acid Synthase 1 -- rate-limiting enzyme in heme pathway; GIVLAARI's target
RNAi RNA interference -- mechanism of gene silencing used by GIVLAARI
GalNAc N-acetylgalactosamine -- ligand for hepatocyte-targeted delivery
OOP Out-of-pocket costs
LTC Long-term complications
ATU Awareness, Trial, and Usage (study type)
PET Promotional Effectiveness Tracker (study type)
OLE Open-label extension (clinical trial design)
LFT Liver function test
GFR Glomerular filtration rate

Average diagnostic delay: Up to 15 years from symptom onset to correct diagnosis

Smoking

Pricing:

US list price: ~$39,000 per vial
Annual treatment cost: ~$575,000 at list; ~$442,000 after discounts
Alnylam offers prevalence-based value agreements: if a payer has more patients than expected, Alnylam provides rebates; in exchange, payers cover GIVLAARI based on FDA label rather than restrictive clinical trial criteria

2.2 PANHEMATIN / NORMOSANG (Hemin) -- The Only Other AHP-Specific Treatment
Mechanism: Hemin (heme for injection) replenishes the depleted heme pool in hepatocytes, providing negative feedback to ALAS1 and reducing ALA/PBG production. It treats the biochemical derangement but does not prevent attacks prophylactically.
Approved indication: PANHEMATIN (US) is indicated for the amelioration of recurrent attacks of acute intermittent porphyria related to the menstrual cycle in susceptible women, after initial carbohydrate therapy is inadequate. FDA has designated it an "essential medicine."
Clinical use patterns (from ZoomRx data):

As needed (acute treatment): Used during attacks to reduce severity and duration. This is the most common use, particularly in Brazil
Prophylactic (off-label): Some HCPs use regularly scheduled hemin infusions to prevent attacks. This off-label use is more common in the US
PANHEMATIN holds ~56% of total patient share in the US across both as-needed and prophylactic use (Wave 4, 2024)

Key limitations vs. GIVLAARI:

Requires IV administration, often in hospital/infusion center setting
No subcutaneous option -- creates access and convenience barriers
Associated with phlebitis, catheter complications, iron overload with repeated use
Not approved for prophylactic use (off-label)
Tachyphylaxis may develop with repeated use

Competitive dynamic: GIVLAARI is gaining share primarily from prophylactic PANHEMATIN users and treatment-naive patients. PANHEMATIN as-needed retains a role for acute attack management and may complement GIVLAARI.
2.3 Other Management Approaches

IV glucose/carbohydrate loading: First-line acute management for mild attacks; suppresses ALAS1 via metabolic feedback. Inadequate for moderate-to-severe attacks
Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues: Used in some women with menstrual-cycle-related attacks to suppress ovulation
Liver transplantation: Curative but reserved for extremely severe, refractory cases with life-threatening complications
Supportive care: Pain management (opioids, often chronic), anti-emetics, seizure management (levetiracetam preferred -- many anticonvulsants are contraindicated)


3. Market Dynamics
3.1 This Is Not a Competitive Market -- It Is a Patient-Finding Market
The fundamental commercial challenge for GIVLAARI is unlike any typical pharma competitive scenario:

No direct competitor: GIVLAARI is essentially first-in-class for prophylactic AHP treatment. There is no competing RNAi or targeted therapy in development for AHP
Indirect competitor (hemin): PANHEMATIN/NORMOSANG is an IV treatment used acutely and sometimes prophylactically, but it has significant convenience and safety disadvantages
Pipeline: The AHP pipeline is sparse. No novel mechanisms are in late-stage development as of 2026
The real "competitor" is non-diagnosis: The vast majority of potential GIVLAARI patients have not been diagnosed with AHP. Every new diagnosis expands the addressable market

3.2 Market Size and Revenue

GIVLAARI + OXLUMO combined revenue: $500 million in FY2025 (up 18% from FY2024)
Q4 2025 combined revenue: $137 million (up 26% YoY)
GIVLAARI patient growth: ~15% year-over-year in 2025
2026 guidance: Mid-single-digit growth for rare disease portfolio
Context: GIVLAARI is a mature rare disease franchise within Alnylam's portfolio. The company's aggressive growth targets (25% CAGR through 2030) are driven by the TTR amyloidosis franchise (AMVUTTRA/ONPATTRO), not GIVLAARI

3.3 Regional Market Differences
ZoomRx runs both US PET and Global ATU (US, Germany, Brazil) for GIVLAARI, providing rich cross-market data:
United States (n=73 HCPs, Wave 4):

Largest market with most established diagnostic infrastructure
HCPs increasingly view AHP as a chronic condition (majority in W4)
Primary treatment objective: preventing long-term complications from elevated ALA/PBG (shift from attack prevention)
GIVLAARI top driver: Efficacy in improving chronic symptoms (59%)
GIVLAARI top barriers: High OOP costs (56%), payer coverage concerns (53%)
GIVLAARI patient share expected to grow +8% in next 12 months
GIVLAARI preferred across all age groups (no age exclusion)
66% community practice, 34% academic
HCP segmentation: Enthusiasts (11%, high load/high share), Trialists (21%, low load/high share), Converters (22%, high load/low share), Inexperienced (47%, low load/low share) -- most volume driven by 32% who are Enthusiasts + Trialists

Germany (n=25 HCPs, Wave 4):

Highest current GIVLAARI patient share across all regions
GIVLAARI usage increased ~14% in Q3 2024
Treatment focus shifted from purely LTC prevention to balanced approach (attacks + LTC)
GIVLAARI top drivers: Ability to reduce attacks (64%), targeted MOA (41%)
GIVLAARI top barriers: Patient refusal (41%), burden of attacks not outweighing monthly dosing (41%)
Barriers related to payer coverage and inclusion criteria significantly decreased
HCPs more sensitive to administration logistic burden
Patient age is a bigger factor -- GIVLAARI preferred for younger patients (18-30)
Proximity to medical care has limited/no influence (48% of HCPs)
GIVLAARI share expected to grow +9% in next 12 months
68% academic, 32% community

Brazil (n=15 HCPs, Wave 4):

Smallest market in the study with significant access challenges
HCPs less likely to consider AHP events without medical intervention as true attacks
HCPs prioritize attack prevention over LTC management (different from US)
GIVLAARI top driver: Ability to significantly reduce attacks (75%)
GIVLAARI top barriers: High OOP costs (58%), payer coverage (58%)
~45% of patients not on consistent treatment due to cost/payer restrictions
Proximity to medical care is a significant influencing factor (40% of HCPs)
GIVLAARI preferred for younger patients (18-30) but hemin as needed preferred for 46+
GIVLAARI patient share expected to remain consistent (~26%)
Cost/access is a structural barrier, not just a commercial one

3.4 HCP Segmentation (US Detail)
ZoomRx ATU Wave 4 identified four distinct HCP segments based on AHP patient load and GIVLAARI patient share:



Segment

Strategic implication: The 47% Inexperienced segment represents the largest conversion opportunity. These HCPs manage few AHP patients and default to hemin as needed. Alnylam's field force should educate them on GIVLAARI's benefits for patients who present with even a single attack, moving beyond the "4+ attacks" mental threshold.
3.5 Patient Types Framework
ZoomRx tracks GIVLAARI usage across five defined patient types:



Patient Type
Definition
GIVLAARI Affinity




Type 1: Recurrent Attacks
4+ attacks requiring healthcare intervention in past 12 months
Highest GIVLAARI share across all regions -- the core treated population


Type 2: Severe History, <2 attacks/year
At least 1 severe/life-threatening attack in past 2 years, but <=2 in last 12 months
Moderate GIVLAARI share; clinical uncertainty about prophylaxis


Type 3: Typical History, <2 attacks/year
At least 1 non-severe attack in past 2 years, <=2 in last 12 months
Lowest prophylactic therapy use; most likely on hemin as needed


Type 4: Chronic Symptoms
No attacks in past 2 years, but chronic AHP symptoms affecting QoL
Growing GIVLAARI share -- this is the expansion frontier, especially in US


Type 5: Biochemically Active
No attacks or known chronic symptoms, but elevated ALA/PBG
Lowest treatment rates; debated whether prophylaxis is warranted



Expansion direction: GIVLAARI's growth trajectory depends on moving beyond Patient Type 1 (recurrent attackers) into Type 2, Type 4, and eventually Type 5. The shift in disease understanding from "episodic attack disease" to "chronic disease with biochemical toxicity" supports this expansion.

4. Promotional Landscape (from PET Data)
4.1 Message Effectiveness
ZoomRx PET tracks GIVLAARI message effectiveness, recall, and motivation to prescribe. Key findings (Q2 2023):
Most effective messages (above rare disease industry average of 58%):

GIV08 (Hemin use reduction): "GIVLAARI reduced average days of hemin use by 70% vs. placebo" -- highest recall and effectiveness
GIV05 (ALA/PBG reduction): "Rapid and sustained reductions in ALA and PBG" -- strong efficacy signal
GIV02 (Attack reduction): "Significant reduction in porphyria attacks" -- high motivation to Rx
GIV03 (Attack-free patients): "50% attack-free vs. 17% placebo" -- compelling comparative data
GIV06 (MOA): "RNAi targeting ALAS1 mRNA" -- appeals to scientifically oriented HCPs

Underperforming messages (below industry average for recall despite high motivation):

GIV11 (Daily worst pain scores) -- highly motivating but poorly recalled, suggesting delivery gap
GIV12 (Analgesic use reduction) -- same pattern

All GIVLAARI messages are rated above industry average for effectiveness in Q2 2023.
4.2 Sales Rep Performance

GIVLAARI reps rated highly above industry standards across all performance attributes
Higher proportion of MDs are promoters of Alnylam (NPS improved Q4 2022 to Q2 2023)
Over 50% of GIVLAARI sales calls are "high-impact details" in Q2 2023
In-person meetings are the most preferred mode of engagement
Efficacy discussions, product brochures, and rep knowledge of AHP drive high-impact interactions
Topics most discussed: efficacy (66%), indication, safety, dosing, patient types, AHP testing/diagnosis

4.3 Key Promotional Implication
Alnylam reps should prioritize:

Leading with chronic symptom efficacy data (top driver in US) rather than just attack reduction
Improving delivery of pain/analgesic data (GIV11, GIV12) which motivate Rx but have low recall
Discussing AHP testing and diagnosis with gastroenterologists specifically (71% of GI docs discuss this vs. 7% of HemOncs)
Targeting Converters and Inexperienced with clinical trial data (% of patients with 0 attacks) to overcome efficacy comparability concerns


5. Key Themes for ZoomRx Deliverables
5.1 Disease Reframing: Attacks to Chronicity
The most significant narrative shift in AHP is the reframing from "episodic attack disease" to "chronic disease with biochemical toxicity." This has direct implications for:

Expanding the treatable patient population (Types 4 and 5)
Supporting GIVLAARI's value beyond attack prevention
Driving long-term treatment adherence
Justifying treatment in patients with fewer attacks but elevated ALA/PBG

5.2 The Diagnostic Gap Is the Market Gap
Every patient found is a patient gained. Alnylam's growth depends on:

Educating PCPs, ER physicians, and GI specialists to test for AHP (urine ALA/PBG)
Reducing the average 15-year diagnostic delay
Genetic cascade testing of family members (supported by HCP belief that family members should be tested)
ZoomRx studies should continue tracking HCP referral patterns and diagnostic behaviors

5.3 Access as the Rate-Limiting Step
Even among diagnosed patients:

US: High OOP costs and payer coverage are top 2 barriers
Brazil: ~45% of patients not on consistent treatment due to cost
The attack-count inclusion criteria create a Catch-22: payers require 4+ attacks, but patients who would benefit may not yet have documented that many attacks
Alnylam's prevalence-based value agreements are a strategic response but need broader adoption

5.4 Global Heterogeneity
US, Germany, and Brazil represent three distinct market archetypes:

US: Largest market, most mature, shifting toward chronic disease management -- growth from Converter/Inexperienced HCP conversion
Germany: Highest GIVLAARI share, academic-dominated, growing share from new patient starts -- growth from continued clinical adoption
Brazil: Access-constrained, cost-dominated, attack-focused rather than chronicity-focused -- growth dependent on structural access improvements
